Walkie-talkie.wikitext: {{topicon Fandom|Walkie-Talkie}} {{interlangbox}} {{autoinfobox item |physics= |audible=When toggling |battery=Yes {{nowrap|(13:40 real-time)}} |primary-use=Send voice (hold) |secondary-use=Power button |tertiary-use= |inspectable=Yes (undocumented) }} == Company Description == {{Terminal|Useful for keeping in touch! Hear other players when the walkie-talkie is in your inventory. Must be in your hand and pressed down to transmit voice.}} == Overview == The '''Walkie-Talkie''' is used to communicate with co-workers beyond the proximity chat radius. It can transmit both voice and text (using the in-game chat) when activated. A very useful item, especially if your unit breaks up to cover a larger area. A fully charged walkie-talkie lasts for 820 seconds when turned on. == Strategy == The walkie-talkie allows players to split up while maintaining communication. Another common strategy is for a player to remain in [[Ship|The Ship]] and use the monitor and [[Computer Terminal]] to warn and aid players who are out gathering [[scrap]]. A fully charged walkie-talkie will last longer than an entire day on a moon, the day being 11 minutes and the walkie-talkie's battery being about 13 minutes. Players with a walkie-talkie that is fully charged at the start of a day can use the battery meter to keep track of time while inside the facility, eg. 50% is roughly 5:00 PM. If someone with a walkie-talkie dies while having it on it will produce static on others walkie-talkies. == Sounds == {{audio gallery | The walkie-talkie turning on|File:WalkieTalkieOn.ogg |br| The walkie-talkie turning off|File:WalkieTalkieOff.ogg |br| Sounds played when releasing the walkie-talkie button|File:WalkieTalkieTransmissionOff.ogg|File:WalkieTalkieTransmissionOff2.ogg|File:WalkieTalkieTransmissionOff3.ogg|File:WalkieTalkieTransmissionOff4.ogg|File:WalkieTalkieTransmissionOff5.ogg |br| Static heard when an employee dies while using a walkie-talkie|File:WalkieTalkieCutOut.ogg |br| Unused sound that would likely have played when the walkie-talkie was not in the active inventory slot while receiving|File:WalkieTalkieTalkingNotHeld.ogg |br| Default easter egg sound for Gordion (Kola.ogg).|File:Kola.ogg |br| Random chance easter egg sound for Gordion (FreeRefillsForever.ogg).|File:FreeRefillsForever.ogg }} == Trivia == * When turning on a walkie-talkie and standing against the Company Building wall on [[Gordion]], either directly across from the ship or on the drill's platform, one of the easter egg sounds can be heard. Kola.ogg plays constantly by default. Every 20 seconds, if a player is closer than 5 units to (-38.51, -7.72, -10.56) and rolls a 3/120 chance, FreeRefillsForever.ogg will play. However, this sound will get cut off after 20 seconds due to it being overwritten by Kola.ogg. {{ref code|WalkieTalkie.SetWallAudioVolume}} This easter egg is hinted at in the log "[[Sigurd_Logs#Screams_-_Sep_13|Screams]]". * The back of the walkie-talkie has a barcode that says "uh why". == History== == References == {{navbox items}}

Shovel.wikitext:
{{topicon Fandom}} {{interlangbox}} {{autoinfobox item |physics= |audible=When striking something |primary-use=Swing shovel (release) |secondary-use= |tertiary-use= |inspectable= }} == Company description == {{terminal| For digging! The Company does not condone violence or harm to local ecosystems. Shovels cannot equip to your utility belt. }} == Overview == The '''Shovel''' can be used to eliminate [[Entities]] or destroy [[Bunker Spider]] webs. Shovels do 1 HP damage to entities. [[Stop sign]]s and [[Yield sign]]s are functionally identical to shovels, but are much heavier. While it appears that you hold it with both hands, it does not make your [[hands full]], so in that sense it is not a two-handed item.
However, it is one of the few equipment items that can't be put in the [[utility slot]]. == Strategy == The shovel will become your best friend as it is your base defense weapon. While it is generally a good idea to not be aggressive and not be reckless, sometimes it can pay off by getting a large threat off the field. See the [[Entities]] article for in depth usage of the shovel on each enemy. == Trivia == In [[Version 73]] and earlier, the Company store's description read "For self-defense!". == History == === [[Version 47]] === * Made shovel collisions more consistent; you can now hit enemies around corners and nearby objects without it blocking the shovel. {{navbox items}}

{{topicon Fandom}}
{{interlangbox}}
{{autoinfobox item

<translate>
<!--T:6-->
The '''TZP-Inhalant''' is a type of [[scrap]] that can be purchased from the [[store]] for {{credits|80}} [[credits]]. It temporarily increases an employee's speed, lowers the burden of their inventory weight, and reduces stamina drain when used by holding down left click. It can be used for 38 seconds before running out.

<!--T:5-->
Inhaling too much results in the employee's vision going red and yellow and their voice becoming higher pitched. With excessive use, their vision will have a smoke effect overlaid.

== Company description == <!--T:1-->

</translate>{{terminal|<translate>
<!--T:2-->
This safe and legal medicine can be administered to see great benefits to your performance on the job! Your ability to move LONG distances while carrying HEFTY objects will be second to none! Warning: TZP gas may impact the brain with extended exposure. Follow instructions manual provided with the canister.


<!--T:3-->
Don't forget to share!
</translate>}}<translate>

== Strategy == <!--T:7-->

<!--T:8-->
The TZP-Inhalant is best used for escaping threats that can normally outrun the employee, such as [[Thumper|Thumpers]], [[Jester|Jesters]], [[Bracken|Brackens]], and [[Eyeless Dog|Eyeless Dogs]].

<!--T:9-->
TZP-Inhalant is also useful for securing a large amount of [[scrap]] before dangerous [[Entities|entities]] become active in a facility.

<!--T:10-->
It is also good for collecting [[Bee Hive|Bee Hives]], as it allows employees to outrun the [[Circuit Bees]] and secure the hive.

== Trivia == <!--T:11-->
</translate>
* <translate><!--T:16--> TZP is short for "Tetrazenylpurine", as printed on the can's label. This chemical does not exist in real life.</translate>
* <translate><!--T:17--> The back of the can has a barcode that says "uh why".</translate>

{{autoinfobox item

<translate>
<!--T:11-->
The '''Zap Gun''' is a type of [[scrap]] that can be purchased from the [[store]] for {{credits|400}} [[credits]]. It is a tool for stunning [[entities]] instead of directly damaging them.

== Company description == <!--T:2-->
</translate>
{{terminal|<translate>
<!--T:3-->
The most specialized self-protective equipment, capable of sending upwards of 80,000 volts!


<!--T:10-->
To keep it targeted as long as possible, pull the gun side-to-side to counter the bend and fight against the pull of the electric beam. It can only stun for as long as you keep the current flowing.
</translate>
}}
<translate>
== Function == <!--T:12--> 

<!--T:5-->
To perform the stun, the employee must keep the current centered on the target by moving it left or right depending on the pull. Failing this will cause the Zap Gun to overheat, disabling usage for a short period of time. The Zap Gun has a limited amount of charge before having to recharge it at the [[Ship#Recharge station|recharge station]] present in the ship.

<!--T:6-->
* When not cranking, the [[Jester]] will remain stunned for a period even after the Zap Gun animation ends.

== Strategy == <!--T:7-->

<!--T:8-->
Simply put, this is a reusable and more sustained [[Stun Grenade]] that is best used in combination with a [[Shovel]]-wielding co-worker to damage the threat while the other stuns it. The Zap Gun is also a good way to stall for your co-workers to run and for your stamina to regenerate. The Zap Gun is able to hit your co-workers (which slows their movement speed to a crawl), so it is best to avoid putting them in your line of fire.

== History== <!--T:9-->
</translate>

Store.wikitext:
{{topicon Fandom}} {{topicon Fandom|Dropship|1}} {{topicon Fandom|Decor|2}} [[Category:Gameplay_mechanics]] {{interlangbox}} [[File:Store.png|thumb| The store]] The '''Store''' is a [[Computer Terminal|terminal]] command which can be used to purchase and deliver various items. == Usage == {{split|Dropship}} The store can be accessed by typing "store" in the ship's terminal. This returns a full list of all the items that can be purchased on that day. Employees are always able to buy any equipment, but ship upgrades and ship decorations have a random rotation of purchasable items that changes every day. Items can be purchased by typing the name of an item and the desired number of that item (e.g. "flashlight 3") into the terminal. If this ship is already on a moon, a drop pod delivers the bought items 17 seconds after purchase (or 27 seconds if this is the first order of the day, and with the caveat that the dropship's timer doesn't start until 8:09 AM when the [[ship]] has landed). Once landed, the drop pod plays a melody so it can be located by employees. Items must be retrieved quickly as the pod leaves after just 30 seconds, regardless of whether the items were dispensed or not. If the ship is in orbit at the time of buying, the drop pod lands on the next moon the employees land on. Ship upgrades and decor and suits can only be purchased once. Attempting to buy a duplicate results in the message This has already been unlocked for your ship! unless your balance is too low, in which case you get the usual out-of-funds message. === Sales === It is possible for items to be on sale, meaning they can be purchased at a reduced price. This can happen randomly and every item has its own maximum sale percentage, this being 80% for most of the items. Item sales are re-rolled every day. == Store items == Items purchasable are split into four categories: equipment, ship upgrades, ship decoration, and suits. === Equipment === {| class="wikitable sortable" ! Name ! Image ! Price (c) ! Weight (lb) |- {{#invoke:Items/StoreEquipmentTable|main}} |} {{#invoke:Navbar|navbar|Module:Items/StoreEquipmentTable|mini=y|style=display: block; margin-top: -1em;}} Employees can also purchase a "survival kit". It consists of [[Flashlight]]s x4, [[Walkie-talkie]]s x4, and a [[Shovel]] x1, and costs {{credits|138}}. (This is just the total price of those items; there is no discount.)
However, in v64 the survival kit is bugged, and will instead give 4 Flashlights, 4 Boomboxes, and a Stun grenade. [[File:Image 2024-08-27 142013752-removebg-preview.png|thumb|link=Company_Cruiser]] === Company Cruiser === {{main|Company Cruiser}} Costs {{credits|400}} {{credits|370}}.
Can be bought for free one time if destroyed or lost.
=== Ship upgrades === {| class="wikitable sortable" ! Name !! Price (c) |- | [[Loud Horn|Loud horn]] || 100 |- | [[Signal Translator|Signal translator]] || 255 |- | [[Teleporter]] || 375 |- | [[Inverse Teleporter|Inverse teleporter]] || 425 |} === Ship decoration === {| class="wikitable sortable" ! Name !! Price (c) |- | [[Cozy lights]] || 140 |- | [[Television]] || 130 |- | [[Toilet]] || 150 |- | [[Shower]] || 180 |- | [[Record player]] || 120 |- | [[Table]] || 80 |- | [[Romantic table]] || 120 |- | [[Jack-o-Lantern]] || 50 |- | [[Welcome mat]] || 40(actually 50) |- | [[Goldfish]] || 50 |- | [[Plushie pajama man]] || 100 |- | [[Disco ball]] || 150 |- | [[Classic painting]] || 400 |- | [[Dog house]] || 80 |- | [[Electric chair]] || 140 |- | [[Fridge]] || 225 |- | [[Microwave]] || 80 |- | [[Sofa chair]] || 150 |} === Suits === {| class="wikitable sortable" ! Name !! Image !! Price (c) |- | [[Green suit]] || [[File:Greensuit.png|center|100x100px|link=Green_suit]] || 60 |- | [[Hazard suit]] || [[File:Yellowsuit.png|center|100x100px|link=Hazard_suit]] || 90 |- | [[Pajama suit]] || [[File:Pajamasuit.png|center|100x100px|link=Pajama_suit]] || 900 |- | [[Purple suit]] || [[File:Purple Suit.png|center|100x100px|link=Purple_suit]] || 70 |- | [[Bee suit]] || [[File:Bee_Suit.webp|center|100x100px|link=Bee_suit]] || 110 |- | [[Bunny suit]] || [[File:Bunny_Suit.webp|center|100x100px|link=Bunny_suit]] || 200 |} == Trivia == * There is an unlisted item in the store called [[mapper tools]], costing {{credits|30}}. Its informational page states it will give employees an overhead view of their surroundings using light-detection; however, purchasing one just gets you a shovel. If spawned in with mods, the tool functions as a portable version of the ship's main monitor. * The welcome mat has an actual cost of {{credits|50}} because of a different configuration between internal assets. == References == [https://pastebin.com/FnddAq8C Related assets] of the welcome mat's actual price

Entities.wikitext:
{{topicon Fandom|Bestiary}} {{topicon Fandom|Entity|1}} {{interlangbox}} [[File:ThumperFunny.png|thumb|A [[Thumper]], an example of an entity found [[interiors|indoors]]]] '''Entities''' are creatures found on [[moons]]. They may be harmless, mildly disruptive, or pose a serious threat to employee safety. There are currently 32 entities that can spawn naturally throughout the game. Entities are grouped into three main categories: * Indoor * Nighttime (Outdoor) * Daytime (Outdoor) Each type has a unique spawning curve that varies by moon. These curves are detailed on each moon's page. For guidance on reading spawning curves, see the [[mechanics]] page. == Entity list == * '''Health:''' The number of health points an entity has. One point equals the damage from a single [[shovel]] hit. A value of ∞ on a green background means the entity can't be harmed through normal means, but can still be defeated (e.g., via an [[Earth Leviathan]]). ∞ on a red background means the entity is entirely invulnerable. * '''Power Level:''' Indicates the entity's power level. See [[Mechanics#Entity_spawning|Entity Spawning Mechanics]] for details. * '''Max Spawned:''' Maximum number of that entity type that can exist simultaneously (excluding defeated ones). * '''Stunnable:''' Whether the entity can be stunned using a [[stun grenade]], [[zap gun]], or [[radar booster]]. === Indoor === Entities that spawn in indoor environments such as the [[Factory]], [[Mansion]], and [[Mineshaft]]. {| class="wikitable sortable" style="width:100%;" ! Name !! Image !! Health !! Power Level !! Diversity Level !! Max Spawned !! (Unused) || [[File:Red pill.png|center|100x100px|link=Red Pill]] | {{#invoke:Entities/AutoHitsToKill|for|RedPillEnemyType|cell-bg=y}} | Unknown || Unknown || style="text-align:center;" | Unknown |} Red Pills lack a proper hitbox and cannot be destroyed, even by traps like the [[Spike Trap]]. == Entity spawn chances == {{#invoke:Moons/AutoTables|entitiesSpawnChancesTables_AutoIndoor|all-moons=1}} {{#invoke:Moons/AutoTables|entitiesSpawnChancesTables_AutoNighttime|all-moons=1}} {{#invoke:Moons/AutoTables|entitiesSpawnChancesTables_AutoDaytime|all-moons=1}} The Maneater's chance to spawn is increased 1.5x ''if the moon's interior is a [[mineshaft]]''. If it is a [[facility]] or [[mansion]], the values listed here are used. == Trivia == {{modded|section=y}} * Lasso Man is impossible to encounter without the use of mods. In V47, an issue likely caused by [https://thunderstore.io/c/lethal-company/p/2018/Brutal_Company/ Brutal Company] existed where the [[Weekly challenge|challenge moon]] during that time ('''Voiumya-10''' on Rend) was able to spawn Lasso Man due to its flag spawningDisabled being set to false. * The Kidnapper Fox was the first entity to be removed from a public version of the game - added in V55, removed in V60, and re-added in V80. == References == {{navbox entities}}

Radar-booster.wikitext: {{topicon Fandom}} {{interlangbox}} {{autoinfobox item |physics= |audible=When PINGed |battery=No |weapon=Yes |primary-use=Turn on booster |secondary-use= |tertiary-use= |inspectable=Yes (undocumented) }} == Company description == {{terminal| Radar boosters come with many uses! Use the "SWITCH" command before the radar booster's name to view it on the main monitor. It must be activated. Use the "PING" command before the radar booster's name to play a special sound from the device. }} == Overview == The '''Radar Booster''' can be purchased in the [[Store]]. When turned on, it can be used as another camera from the [[Ship#Terminal|terminal]]. It also appears when an employee scans (right clicks). The radar booster will be assigned a random name the first time it is turned on. The assigned name can then be filled into the [RADAR BOOSTER NAME] mentioned below. All the possible names are: ''Betty, George, Billy, Kablam, Susie, Igor, Gilbert, Daryl, Seth, Sam, Brody, Blue, Freddy, Sam, Bobbie, Louie, Albert'' But if the radar booster wasn't turned on since purchased, it will stay "Unnamed" while scanning, and can neither be viewed on the ship camera, nor be selected from the terminal command. == Available commands == The following commands are only available on the [[Ship#Terminal|ship terminal]], and it requires the target radar booster to be turned on beforehand by holding it and clicking LMB. [[File:GhostDevicePing.ogg|thumb| Radar booster ping sound]] PING [RADAR BOOSTER NAME] This command will cause the radar booster to emit a "hello" sound, helping guide employees to its location or attract sound-sensitive entities to it. [[File:RadarBoosterFlash.ogg|thumb| Radar booster flash sound]] FLASH [RADAR BOOSTER NAME] This command will cause the radar booster to flash. The effects are similar to a [[stun grenade]] exploded in the radar booster's position, except there's no [[damage]] to the employees standing at the center, and the stun time has only 1.75 seconds at base. {{#invoke:Entities/AutoStunTimeTable|radar_booster}} Despite the short stun time, this flashing ability allows for some very interesting tactics because of its reusability and portability. == Strategy == Employees can place the radar booster right at the main entrance of indoors to act as a beacon in case an employee gets lost. It can also be placed to relocate loot or keep watch for enemies. It could alternatively act as a long lasting light source while held, but it also consistently occupies the right side of view, making it dangerous if there's hostile entities coming from your right side. === Doorway guardian === A radar booster can be placed right in front of the ship doorway. It can be used to repeatedly flash incoming [[Forest Keeper|forest keepers]], clearing a path for outside employees, or ping to guide lost employees back to their right track. === Flashbang strategy === The flash command gives an employee a way of repeatedly disabling a creature. Due to its fairly short cooldown, lack of maximum uses, and the fact that the booster can be easily moved around, one could easily set up an ambush for [[Thumper]]s (or any other indoor entity), making their disposal significantly easier. One does have to be wary of the fact that the blinding flash does also blind and slow the employee. A radar booster can be given to a [[Hoarding Bug]] to locate its nest. Additionally, the flash command may be used to disable any guarding Hoarding Bugs. The employee could also carry the radar booster with them on maps where forest keepers are particularly prevalent, as the Forest Keeper can be stunned with the radar booster's flash. If the command is timed well enough, the employee can make an easy escape. The command also disables [[Coil-Head]]s for a brief period of time, allowing the employee to turn around and look for an escape. == History == === [[Version 45]] === * Added flash command. * Increased cost to 60 scrap. === [[Version 40]] === Pinging and camera monitoring functional, purchasable in store. === Version 35 === A placeholder RadarBoosterItem class included, but the radar booster cannot actually function. == Sounds == {{audio gallery | Turning a radar booster on|File:GhostDeviceFullyCharged.ogg |br| Turning a radar booster off|File:GhostDeviceTurnOff.ogg |br| A radar booster dropped onto the ground|File:GhostDeviceHitGround.ogg |br| The humming sound when a radar booster is turned on|File:GhostDeviceHum.ogg |br| The humming sound when a radar booster is turned on (Repeated 5 times, +2dB)|File:RadarBoosterHumRepeated.ogg }} == Trivia == * The radar booster's model comes from one of Zeekerss' other games, [https://store.steampowered.com/app/1349060/It_Steals/ It Steals]. == Notes == The name Sam is in the list twice, meaning it's chosen twice as often as the others. {{navbox items}}

Weather.wikitext: {{topicon Fandom}} {{interlangbox}} {{Verify}} Weather is a mechanic intended to add difficulty on [[moons]]. Players can check the forecast on a moon through the text in brackets beside each moon's name within the ‘moons’ terminal command, provided the day has fully changed. Weather does not affect the amount and value of [[scrap]]. == Weather types == === Eclipsed === [[File:Eclipsedmoon.png|thumb| A moon experiencing an eclipse]] This weather phenomenon allows for immediate spawns of nighttime entities and significantly increases the spawn rate of entities both indoors and outdoors, making eclipsed moons much more dangerous to explore with no additional benefit. During an eclipse, the minEnemiesToSpawn(indoor entities) and minOutsideEnemiesToSpawn(nighttime outdoor entities) are changed according to the table below. See [[Mechanics]] for a detailed explanation of these two variables. {| class="wikitable ! ! minEnemiesToSpawn ! minOutsideEnemiesToSpawn |- | [[Experimentation]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 1 |- | [[Assurance]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 1 |- | [[Vow]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 1 |- | [[Offense]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 4 |- | [[March]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 2 |- | [[Adamance]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 3 |- | [[Rend]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 3 |- | [[Dine]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 4 |- | [[Titan]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 3 |- | [[Embrion]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 4 |- | [[Artifice]] || colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | 3 |} === Flooded === The moon begins to flood, making it impossible to traverse its exterior after a certain point. Some moons, such as Adamance, start at maximum flooding, with the water level lowering over the course of the day. Moving on flooded terrain slows employees down considerably and makes them unable to sprint. It also drains stamina, preventing it from regenerating until the employee is out of the water. Being underwater for a prolonged period of time drowns the employee. Flooding is based on the time of the day and progresses as time goes on, making the moon more dangerous to navigate the longer that employees stay. Time until Maximum Flood level: Approx. 5 pm, may vary between moons. === Foggy === {{about|the outdoor weather type|fog found indoors|Interiors#Indoor fog}} The entire moon's exterior is covered in thick fog, making it difficult to navigate. There is a mechanism for reducing [[entities]]' vision in fog: The maximum line-of-sight distance for spotting employees is capped at 30 units (you can think of this as making everything near-sighted). All entities are affected by this as it's managed by the base class EnemyAI. It does also allow for the clamping step to be skipped with a property canSeeThroughFog, but no entity uses that. === Rainy === Rain leaves dark patches of quicksand on the ground, posing a swift and lethal threat to employees. It is possible to escape the quicksand by reacting quickly and moving out before going too deep. Sinking too deep will result in the employees death which will leave no body and delete all held items. A quicksand patch looks a bit like a large circular puddle of brown dirt on the ground and especially at night, it can be quite hard to spot it. March, which cannot have rainy weather, and Adamance have permanently fixed quicksand patches on the ground regardless of the weather conditions. === Stormy === [[File:LightningSparkEffect.gif|thumb|200px| A sparking item]] [[File:StormStaticElectricity.ogg|thumb|200px| The sound played when an item is sparking]] During a thunderstorm, conductive items lying on the ground or held by employees can acquire an electric charge that causes them to be struck by lightning after a short delay. When this happens, the item audibly begins to spark. Lighting strikes can be avoided by dropping the item and moving away. Lightning deals 50 damage to instant death to the player depending on distance to the strike. Random lightning strikes can also occur around the map independently of conductive items and may strike employees without warning. * If an employee has a conductive item in their inventory but is not actively holding it, sparks will still appear and they can identify the charged item by rotating through inventory slots. * Lightning may strike a metal object multiple times, so it is advised to wait for a second or two before picking the items back up. * If a sparking item is located below a cover such as a roof, the lightning bolt will instead strike on the roof and not hit the item directly. This mainly works on solid roofs or the ship's ceiling. There might be other spots where this works depending on the map, but this is not always a reliable strategy. * If lightning strikes the ship, the ship's lights and monitor will turn off. They can be turned on again with the light switch or a [[Remote]], and the power button on the side of the monitor, respectively. * It is possible for employees to avoid lightning to some extent by standing close to a wall when holding a sparking item, or by having a tulip snake on them. * The "Lightning strike warning" cannot appear when an object in an employee's inventory is sparking. * Items bought during Stormy weather are not conductive and stay that way until the ship enters orbit again. For a full list of conductive items, check the [[scrap]] page and also the [[Store#Equipment|equipment list]], or take a look at the [[:Category:Conductive items|conductive items category]]. == Weather choice == Firstly, the game decides how many moons will be affected by weather. This value is evaluated from a curve with a random input value between 0 and 1. If the following criteria are met, the evaluated value is multiplied by a random number between 1.5 and 2.5: * The crew consists of at least 2 employees (Multiplayer) * The number of days all employees survived in a row without dying is a multiple of 3 (at least 3 days) Note: The evaluated value is converted to an integer and clamped between 0 and the number of moons, which means that if the value is above 8, all moons will be affected by weather. The curve on the left is the default curve for determining how many moons will be affected by weather conditions. The curve on the right is multiplied by 2.5, the maximum possible multiplier, used when the above criteria are met. [[File:Moonweathercurvegraph.png|950px]] For every moon that has been selected, a random index in the list of the moon's possible weather then determines the type of weather the moon will have on that day. == See also == * [[Meteor shower]] == Notes ==

Ship.wikitext: {{topicon Fandom|The_Ship}} {{topicon Fandom|Monitors|1}} {{topicon Fandom|Terminal|2}} {{topicon Fandom|Storage|3}} {{topicon Fandom|Electric_Coil|4}} {{topicon Fandom|Intercom|5}} {{topicon Fandom|Bunkbeds|6}} {{topicon Fandom|File_Cabinet|7}} {{interlangbox}} {{Infobox Furniture | name = Ship | image = Ship Transparent.webp | defaultprice = 0 }} The '''Autopilot''' '''Ship''' is where every crew begins their run in ''Lethal Company'', and is their base of operations and overall safest location. However, several entities can enter the ship; [[Eyeless Dog]]s, [[Baboon Hawk]]s, [[Kidnapper Fox]]es, [[Maneater]]s, [[Ghost Girl]], [[Circuit Bees]], [[Masked]], [[Giant Sapsucker]]s, [[Cadaver Bloom]]s, and [[Feiopar]]s are all capable of entering. The door is at the aft (back) of the ship and the monitors at the fore (front). This is confirmed by a line in the [[clipboard|"Training manual" clipboard]]: [...] the electric coil located on the right [''recte'' starboard] interior wall of the ship [...] Thus the ship takes off forwards, and reverses in to land. == Included utilities == By default, the ship contains a main monitor, a terminal, a brake lever, a recharge station, four secondary monitors, door controls, a light switch, a cupboard, a filing cabinet, and a bunk bed. === Main monitor === The screen gives an employee a simplistic top-down view of the employee they are observing, with the following symbols representing various things: [[File:EntityRadarPipSizes.png|thumb|300px| Examples of red circle sizes (a.k.a. radar pip size) for different entities]] * Blue Circle: Player characters. The cone in front of them is their field of vision. * Yellow/Green Triangle: Scrap items that can be sold. * Blue Line: A fire exit (small blue line) or the main entrance (large blue line). * Blue Line With Yellow Dots: Represents a locked door that needs a key to open. * Red Circle: A nearby creature. Different sizes of circles represent different entities. However, the circle's behavior and speed (and players' reactions to it) is the most important factor in deciding which entity it represents. * Swarm of Tiny Gray Dots: [[Circuit Bees]] (outdoors) or [[Mask Hornets]] (indoors). * Red Square/Triangle: Represents landmines and turrets respectively. * Red Line: Represents a vent. * Letter with a Number: A code associated with the equipment it controls. Can be over secure doors, [[landmine]]s, [[turret]]s, and [[Spike Trap|spike trap]]s. Typing the codes into the terminal will open/close the door or temporarily disable landmines, turrets, and spike traps. The white button to the left of the Monitor switches perspectives between different players. The red button below it turns the main monitor on and off. The main monitor can also be viewed from the terminal by using the "VIEW MONITOR" command, and the perspective can be switched with the "SWITCH" command. Before landing on a moon, the main monitor displays flavor text about the moon that is currently selected. Depending on the text's length, it may also display the moon's current weather. === Brake lever === The '''Brake lever''' allows employees to land the ship on the moon they are orbiting, as well as allowing them to start the ship when they have landed to end the day and go back into orbit. While employees frequently land on Gordion on the final day (Day 0) to sell and meet quota, maximizing profit, it is possible to land and sell there earlier. When the ship lands on Gordion and nothing is sold, the day counter does not decrease. It is also possible to land on a moon other than Gordion on the final day, which always results in the quota being failed and subsequent disciplinary action. === Terminal === The Computer Terminal has a variety of functions, including navigation, shopping, and viewing bestiary entries. Commands are ''not'' case sensitive. Some commands only depend on the first 3 letters; for example, "ada", "adasdfghj", "adama", etc. will be recognized as the command for "adamance". The following commands can be used on the terminal: {| class="wikitable sortable" ! Input !! Result |- |'''HELP''' | Displays the first page of commands. |- |'''OTHER''' | Displays the second page of commands. |- |'''MOONS''' | Allows players to choose between [[moons]] to land in and observe weather conditions for all moons. |- |'''STORE''' | Opens the [[Store]]. |- |'''BESTIARY''' | Opens the [[Bestiary]]. |- |'''STORAGE''' | Accesses objects placed into storage. |- |'''EJECT''' | Gently retires the employees in the ship, used to reset runs (command can only be done by Host). |- |'''VIEW MONITOR''' | Toggles on and off the main monitor's map camera. |- |'''SWITCH [Player name]''' | Switches view on the map camera to the specified player. Switches in order when no player name is specified. |- |'''PING [Radar booster name]''' | Makes the specified radar booster play a sound. |- |'''FLASH [Radar booster name]''' | Makes the specified radar booster flash at anyone looking at it, stunning enemies. |- |'''TRANSMIT [message]''' | Puts a message on everyone's screen if the [[Signal Translator]] has been bought. |- |'''SCAN''' | Returns the amount of items left on the current moon. |- |'''SIGURD''' | Opens up the [[Sigurd Logs]]. |} Commands can be copied from the chat into the terminal to allow quick access to a specific command. [[File:Ship coil.png|thumb|150px| The coil]] === Coil === {{redirect|Coil|the interior entity|Coil-head}} The coil can be used to recharge battery-powered objects, allowing them to be reused after their battery is depleted. [[Walkie-talkie]]s, [[Flashlight]]s, [[Pro-flashlight]]s, [[Zap gun]]s, [[Boombox]]es, [[Jetpack]]s, [[Hairdryer]]s, and [[Laser pointer]]s are rechargeable using this method. {{clear}}[[File:Ship monitors.png|thumb|250px]] === Secondary monitors === There are four monitors outside of the main monitor. The rightmost monitor shows a camera pointed out the back of the ship. It can be used to see threats that may be coming towards the ship, including the [[Ghost Girl]]. The top-rightmost monitor shows the inside of the ship at a very low resolution, thus serving a mostly cosmetic purpose. There are then two monitors above the main monitor - the leftmost shows the employees' quota, and the other shows the amount of days left until quota is due. The quota monitor also shows the total amount employees have sold after they sell at the company building on [[Gordion]]. The days left monitor always counts down from three, showing "NOW" once the ship has taken off from the moon left on the final day. {{clear}}[[File:Door_Controls.png|thumb|300px| The door controls]] === Door controls === The door controls are used to open and temporarily close the ship's exit door. It has two buttons and a display. Pressing the green button with a vertical bar opens the door, while pressing the red one with a circle closes the door. The display is used to show the percentage on how long the door will stay closed. When the percentage reaches 0%, the door opens, and it takes around 6 seconds to reach 100% again. When started on 100%, the door stays closed for about 30 seconds. There is also a small monitor screen above the door controls that shows what is going on outside the door. It is useful to keep [[Eyeless Dog]]s and [[Masked]] out of the ship for a time, and to prevent Eyeless Dogs from being alerted to talking or dropping items inside the ship. === Light switch === The light switch turns the lights on or off. This is useful in the event that lightning strikes the ship, as it forces the lights off, and an employee will need to manually turn them back on. A [[Remote]] can also be used to turn the lights on and off. If an employee has bought [[Cozy Lights]] and/or the [[disco ball]], they will be turned on in the event that the light switch is turned off. {{clear}}[[File:Ship magnet and lever.png|thumb|200px| The magnet (orange box) and the lever (blue circle)]] === Magnet === On the outside of the ship, on the side without railing, is a magnet that holds the [[Company Cruiser]] to the ship so that it may be taken into orbit. It is turned on and off with a lever located on the outside corner of the ship, accessible by standing on the railing at the end opposite the ship door. Once the Cruiser is attached, nothing can dislodge it other than turning the magnet off again. === Decorations === The three starting decorations are a red cupboard, a filing cabinet, and a bunk bed. Additional decorations may be bought and placed inside the ship from the [[Store]]. The cupboard may be used as storage for tools or scrap items, and the filing cabinet and bunk bed are largely cosmetic (though can be used to store tools and scrap on top of it). Furniture placed "into storage" is in fact moved far outside the map, to the fixed point (800, -100, 0). === Store upgrades === More information about store upgrades can be found on the [[Store]] page. == Bugs == === Clipping === The employee can clip through the ship with the help of the bunk beds or cupboards.{{cite YouTube|video-id=eOe3xhf9NpY|title=How to Clip through the Ship's Ceiling and Floor in Lethal Company(v45)|creator=Alt 1}} Note that some of these are inconsistent in multiplayer, and doing this while in space will teleport the employee back inside if they succeed. To clip through the Cupboards, press B to start "placing" them in order to go inside, then jump and press E or scroll to cancel the placement. If on top of the cupboard and partially in the ceiling, then jumping will cause the employee to be clipped out of bounds. While easier to complete, it is less consistent in multiplayer and can result in needing to be rescued from the wall of the ship. Bunk Beds can be utilized in the same method to clip out of bounds. It is more difficult to pull off, but is also more consistent. Canceling placement without jumping can cause the employee to be clipped through the floor or wall, with varying consistency that might require a rescue. === Corner spot === The left corner of the ship (while facing towards the brake lever) is considered “outside”, meaning standing there and yelling can act as a pseudo loud horn, and Eyeless Dogs will rush outside the ship next to where the corner is are; however, standing too close to the wall can also allow them to attack through it. This can also be used as a safe spot; by angling the terminal in a way where it completely blocks off access to this corner by walking, staying completely silent will make dogs unable to kill the employee. This corner can also be used as a safe spot against the Kidnapper Fox. By placing the filing cabinet in this corner and standing on top of it against the corner when the Fox tries to sneak onto the ship, it will be lured to the outside wall, allowing it to easily be killed by crouching and hitting it. == Notes and references == {{notes and reflist}} {{navbox furniture}}

Interiors.wikitext: {{topicon Fandom|Interior}} {{topicon Fandom|Cabinet|1}} {{topicon Fandom|Elevator|2}} {{topicon Fandom|Door|3}} {{topicon Fandom|Fire_Exit|4}} {{topicon Fandom|Secure_Door|5}} {{topicon Fandom|Breaker_Box|6}} {{interlangbox}} {{split|Factory}} {{split|Mansion}} '''Interior''' refers to the part of a [[moon]]'s surface which lies beyond the entrances—indoors, and usually underground. The hazards present here are distinct from the exterior's. To meet quota, employees must venture inside to gather [[scrap]]. == Interior types == === The Factory === [[File:Factorymainentrance.webp|thumb| The main entrance of the Factory]] [[File:FactoryFan.png|thumb| The large fan]] The Factory is the most common interior type, mostly found on free moons. It consists mainly of labyrinthine concrete hallways and corridors, storage rooms, and grated catwalks. ==== Rooms ==== * '''Main entrance:''' The first room employees enter into, consisting of a grated flooring and large fan spinning above. * '''Catwalks:''' Narrow grated platforms lined with metal railing raised above a deep pit. * '''Broken catwalks:''' Two catwalks with broken railing separated by a large gap. A bent metal beam spans halfway across the gap allowing employees to jump from one catwalk to the other. * '''Staircases:''' Grated staircases raised above a pit allowing traversal of both floors of the Factory. * '''Hallways:''' Sporadically lit concrete hallways arranged in a maze-like configuration. * '''Hallway intersection room:''' A large hexagon shaped room with multiple routes leading to more hallways. Occasionally features a large boiler in the center of the room. * '''Storage rooms:''' Large concrete rooms with shelves throughout and occasionally having a staircase leading downwards. * '''Server rooms:''' Similar to storage rooms, but with dense server racks located throughout the middle. * '''Liminal office room:''' A room with off-white floors and wallpaper, likely a nod to the internet [[w:Creepypasta|creepypasta]] "[[w:The_Backrooms|The Backrooms]]". * '''Locker room:''' A large room with a rectangular pit in the middle, as well as stairs leading up to a balcony overlooking the room. Up to two openable lockers can be found lining the walls underneath the balcony's stairs or in the opposite corner. * '''Manufacturing room:''' An extremely large room featuring four paths deviating from a shared platform. Two paths lead to a large narrow section of the room separated by a pit, the other leads to another narrow area with a conveyor chute lining a wall, while the final path is a catwalk that extends to another room. * '''Apparatus room:''' A room with shelves, a large power machine, and a socket in the wall next to it containing the [[Apparatus]]. === The Mansion === [[File:Mansionmainentrance.webp|thumb| The main entrance of the Mansion interior type]] The Mansion is primarily found on paid moons, specifically [[Dine]] and [[Rend]], but can appear on any moon as a small chance, excluding March. It consists mainly of grand carpeted hallways and rooms adorned with bookshelves, fireplaces, and other household furniture. ==== Rooms ==== * '''Main entrance:''' The first room employees enter into, consisting of a large foyer with stairs leading up to a second floor balcony overlooking the room. Occasionally spawning with bookshelves on the ground floor. * '''Library:''' A room with bookshelves and a staircase allowing traversal of both floors of the Mansion. * '''Fireplace room:''' A room with a fireplace. * '''"Window" room:''' A room featuring a screen displaying a farmland scenery. * '''Staircases:''' A room with a staircase leading upwards. There are two types of rooms devoted to staircases, one which goes straight down and has a closet under the stairs which can contain scrap, and one which wraps around the wall and has a landing midway. * '''Kitchen:''' A room with checkered flooring and kitchen essentials such as a fridge, table, and oven. * '''Greenhouse:''' A room with a long row of plants, a countertop with a sink, and screens on the wall and ceiling similar to those in the "window" room, projecting the appearance of a countryside landscape at twilight. Doors leading into or out of the conservatory are white with inset windows. * '''Bathroom:''' A room with a tiled floor similar to that of the kitchen, a counter with a sink, drawers, and cupboards in which items can be found, a toilet, a shower stall, and a bathtub. * '''Bedroom:''' A room with a double bed, a bedside table, a dresser, a trash can, and a closet. * '''Party room:''' A room with a dining table and decorations for a birthday party, including floating balloons. * '''Alcove room:''' A room consisting of a square or C-shaped narrow hallway wrapping around an inaccessible space. Two of the walls on the inner side will have alcoves in which scrap can potentially be found. * '''Pool room:''' A room containing a swimming pool. There is a pink inflatable pool floatie that bobs up and down in the water when jumped on, and the deep end is deep enough to submerge a standing employee. Items can sometimes be found in the pool. * '''Garage:''' A room with a parked car, tool racks on the walls, some cabinets, a recycling bin, and a large pile of cardboard boxes. ==== Strategy ==== To avoid or kill [[entities]] within the Mansion, players can: * Jump on top of the large bookshelves found in the main entrance. (Entities can make it on top of these bookshelves, so the only use for this is to jump to another disjointed bookshelf in the main room. This will cause the entity to either fall in between the bookshelves or go all the way around to try and kill the player.) * Jump on top of large tables. * Jump on top of kitchen counters. === Mineshaft === [[File:MinesEntrance.png|thumb|Entrance to the Mineshaft]] {{main|Mineshaft}} The Mineshaft is an interior added in the [[v60]] update. This interior is most common on [[Vow]], [[Offense]], [[Dine]], and [[Artifice]] but can appear on any moon (except [[March]]). It contains an elevator down to the main Mineshaft area, which will lead to its main area of profit: A deep cave system. === Office === {{unused}} An office covered in leaves. == Interior features == [[File:Fireexit.png|thumb| A fire exit inside a Factory]] === Fire exits === Every building has at least 1 fire exit, which will lead into a random part of the structure. It can lead into (seemingly) any room, replacing an existing space for a doorway. These are 2-way doors like the main entrance. The locations of the fire exit(s) in the exterior aren't randomized. The articles for each moon include maps with the fire exit locations, for example [[March]] with its unique 3 fire exits. Locations in specific interiors: * In [[Factory|Factories]], fire exits will only open into the various catwalk tiles or one of the small rectangular or L-shaped loot rooms. * In [[Mansion]]s, fire exits will only open into one of the tiles with bookshelves. Sometimes obscured by a bookshelf, though the red glow can still be seen. * In [[Mineshaft]]s, fire exits lead straight into the hallways, bypassing the elevator. They will never open into the caves. Due to how the procedural generation of the interior works, fire exits are frequently segmented off from the main entrance by a locked door.{{cite bug|Entering fire exit often leads to locked doors and little else}} === Security doors === Found only in the Factory, security doors can be opened and closed using the [[Ship#Terminal|terminal]] aboard the ship. When power is lost, either through a [[#Breaker panel|breaker panel]] or the removal of the apparatus, all security doors will permanently open. === Vents === Every interior contains multiple small vents lined along walls. Entities spawn out of vents, making noise as they are about to crawl out. Vents appear as red lines along the walls of the building when viewed on the ship's monitor. In Mineshafts, the usual grille vents appear in concrete walls, while black cracks appear in rock walls. File:Vent.png| A vent on a Factory wall File:Less_vent_more_crag.webp| An example of the rock "vents" exclusive to the Mineshaft [[File:Breakerbox.png|thumb| A breaker panel with all switches flipped on]] === Breaker panel === The Breaker Panel can be opened to reveal 5 switches, which can be turned to disable or enable power in certain areas of the facility. While it can be used to open and close security doors, it is recommended to just flip them all on, pointing left, when found to allow better visibly for everyone exploring. While on, it will make a low pitched mechanical humming noise alerting players to its location. === Fan === The large fan above the main entrance of the Factory is more than just a decoration. If any employee flies up to it using a [[jetpack]] or [[Tulip Snake]]s, they will immediately be killed by the high-speed rotating fan blades. === Steam leaks === Found in the Factory and Mineshaft, steam leaks obscure player vision in hallways similar to [[Weather#Foggy|fog]], only dissipating when turning a valve found nearby. Leaks can be present at the start of the day, and valves can also blow during the day. Players continuously shaking their camera while staring at walls within the steam will have increased visibility and a higher chance of finding valves. An employee with [[full hands]] cannot close valves, they must drop their two-handed item temporarily. === Traps === {{main|Traps}} Traps are manmade hazards found indoors, all posing a great threat to employee safety. === Indoor fog === There is a 2% chance every day (or 20% for [[Mechanics#Entity_Infestations|infestation days]]) for the interior to be shrouded in thick fog. Unlike steam leaks, this fog will persist through the entire day and cannot be removed. == References ==

Quota.wikitext: {{topicon Fandom|Profit_Quota}} {{interlangbox}} {{Stub}} The Company establishes a '''Quota''', the minimum amount of credits that the crew must earn by selling [[scrap]] at the Company headquarters on [[Gordion]]. Displayed above the monitor in the [[ship]], players are informed of the amount of credits, how many they have already earned, and the number of days remaining to fulfill it. Players always start each run with a balance of {{credits|60}}. Failure to meet this deadline will lead to the crew being fired and ejected into space, wiping all progress made on that save. While the deadline to reach Quota is three days, players may travel to Gordion sooner if they manage to meet it early. Players need not head back on the day of the deadline when they meet the quota, and any extra scrap can be saved for the next, assuming that at least one player survives on the crew's visit to the next moon. As of [[version 40]], the function SetNewProfitQuota() inside TimeOfDay.cs contains the relevant code to calculate newer minimums after the initial starting quota, as well as the Overtime Bonus. The resulting explanations below were based on that version of the game and may have been adjusted since. == New quota calculation == :''Syntax Note:'' The ∈ symbol only represents the bounds of the equation result. The new quota calculation is governed by a set of three equations. The function first calculates a ''Time Multiplier'', essentially the square of how many times players fulfill the quotas, in a range limited from 0 to 104. (Time Multiplier = 1 + \frac{QuotasFulfilledTimes^2}{16}) \in [0, 10^4] The game then introduces randomization via a curve. Randomizer Offset = 100 \times Time Multiplier \times (1 + EvaluateCurve(Random(-0.503,0.503))) Then the new quota is registered by adding the old quota and the randomized offset. (New Profit Quota = Old Quota + Randomizer Offset) \in [0, 10^9] == Overtime Bonus == An Overtime Bonus rewards the crew for quickly completing their quota ahead of the deadline, or for going above and beyond the quota's value. It is governed by the following equation. OvertimeBonus = \frac{ScrapSold - Quota}{5} + 15 \cdot DaysUntilDeadline Currently, the amount of Overtime Bonus received upon reaching the Quota seems to be 15 short of what the equation would suggest, which might be the result of the DaysUntilDeadline variable being set to -1 when selling with zero days left on the terminal. With this in mind, you should expect to subtract 15 when selling right at the deadline mark. The bonus equation changes when players have zero days left in the deadline: OvertimeBonus = \frac{Scrap Sold - Quota}{5} - 15 To calculate how much scrap to sell to reach a precise total value of credits, calculate: SoldScrap = \frac{5 \cdot WantedCredits + Quota + 75}{6}

Whoopie cushion.wikitext:
{{topicon Fandom|Whoopie-Cushion}}
{{interlangbox}}
{{autoinfobox item}}
{{Stub}}
<translate>
<!--T:1-->
The Whoopie Cushion, introduced in [[Version 45]] of the game, stands out as a unique (and amusing) item. Functioning like any standard item, players can locate and acquire it during gameplay. Notably, the Whoopie Cushion holds no weight within the inventory, making it easier to transport.

<!--T:2-->
Upon being walked over, the Whoopie Cushion emits a distinctive fart sound. It's '''essential''' to note that players are unable to activate the fart sound voluntarily when the item is held, it must be dropped and walked over. While the Whoopie Cushion may not yield significant value in terms of scrap, its appeal lies in its comedic value.

<!--T:3-->
It is especially important to note that the Whoopie Cushion can be hidden within ship upgrades such as the [[Teleporter]] pad or a strategically placed [[welcome mat]], by first placing the Whoopie cushion on the the floor of the ship then moving the upgrade onto it. This can be used to hide one or more Whoopie Cushions with which to "improve" crew morale.

<!--T:4-->
The Whoopie Cushion can also be laid around the ship and stepped on to attract dogs, which will act as a permanent dog trap, as the dog will dash at the whoopie cushion, step on it and make noise, and then repeat the cycle forever.
</translate>

{{topicon Fandom|Spray_Paint}}
{{interlangbox}}
{{autoinfobox item
|name=Spray paint (can)
|physics=<!--normal-->
|audible=While spraying<!-- TODO what about shaking? -->

|inspectable=<!--no-->
}}
== {{section heading/Company description}} ==
<translate>
<!--T:1-->
None present.

== Overview == <!--T:2-->

<!--T:3-->
'''Spray paint''' is a tool item that can be used to paint walls and floors, as well as other surfaces such as on-ship furniture items and even other employees. A can of spray paint must be shaken before it can be used- otherwise, the paint will dissipate into the air instead of being applied. Paint cannot be used infinitely, and a can will eventually run out of paint after about 30 seconds of applying it to a surface (including time taken shaking the can). Paint also comes in four different colors, those being red, yellow, green, and purple; spray paint color is randomly generated upon landing at a moon, and all spray paint purchased on that moon will be the same color.
</translate>
== {{section heading/Strategy}} ==
<translate>
<!--T:4-->
Spray paint can be effectively used as a "breadcrumb trail", allowing an employee to easily mark doorways or walls that lead back to facility entrances or fire exits. Additionally, it can also be used to outline dangerous rooms (i.e rooms with [[Traps|traps]]), as well as dead-ends and explored areas to prevent casualties or wasting time respectively. Employees should make sure to clearly communicate to fellow employees what a certain spray paint marking means so as to prevent confusion and use the tool effectively.
Spray-paint can also be applied to other employees, allowing potential easy identifying of [[Masked]].
</translate>
== {{section heading/History}} ==

=== {{section heading/Version X|45}} ===
<translate>
<!--T:5-->
Introduced.
</translate>
=== {{section heading/Version X|47}} ===
<translate>
<!--T:6-->
Spray paint should now always disappear from the moon when you enter orbit.
</translate>
== {{section heading/Trivia}} ==
<translate>
<!--T:7-->
Color of the paint seems to be randomly selected for every employee.

<!--T:8-->
Some employees may see more paint than others.
</translate>

Shotgun.wikitext:
{{topicon Fandom|Double-barrel}} {{interlangbox}} {{autoinfobox item |physics=No, but see info on misfires |audible=When fired |force-show-conductive=y |primary-use=Fire |secondary-use=Turn safety [on/off] |tertiary-use=Reload |inspectable= }} The '''Shotgun''' is a [[scrap]] item that also functions as a weapon. It does not generate naturally, and is instead only wielded and dropped by [[Nutcracker]]s, along with however many [[Ammo|shells]] the Nutcracker had in the shotgun when it died, with 2 extra shells dropped alongside it. It has a fixed sell value of {{credits|60}} regardless of how many shells are loaded. == Mechanics == When fired, the Shotgun computes two hitscans{{cite YouTube|video-id=wdlQYrd3dio|title=Lethal Company: Shotguns, Shovels and Knives|creator=alter ego|start-timestamp=5:08}} to work out damage dealt to [[entities]], and separately to employees: * Entities that are inside an ellipsoid (stretched ball) area around and in front of the shotgun will be dealt 5 or 3 damage at close range, or 2 damage at mid range. The distance at which a shot will do no damage is fairly close, about the length of the [[Cruiser]]. * Employees who are inside a cone-shaped area emanating forward from the barrels will be dealt 100 [[damage]] (insta-kill) at close or mid range, or at farther ranges, 40, 20, or no damage. This hitbox applies whether the gun is fired by a [[Nutcracker]] or by a fellow employee. * Again, these are ''hitscans'', despite the visuals. Multiple entities can be damaged in a single shot and it has nothing to do with the particles. Shots are blocked by most—but not all—world geometry and objects. ** Notably, catwalks in the [[Factory]] are transparent to the shotgun.{{cite YouTube|video-id=wdlQYrd3dio|title=Lethal Company: Shotguns, Shovels and Knives|creator=alter ego|start-timestamp=6:28}} ** The hitscan doesn't take crouching into consideration. That is to say, crouching won't make the shot less likely to hit you, and it won't reduce the damage you take, unless of course it's to duck your head behind something solid. It has 3 unique controls: * Pressing {{keybind|Item Secondary use}} will turn the safety on and off, stopping the gun from accidentally firing. * Pressing {{keybind|Item primary use}} will cause it to fire one of the two shells. * Pressing {{keybind|Item Tertiary use}} will cause it to load a shell from the inventory into the shotgun if one is there, with a maximum of 2 shells per shotgun. If you meet the conditions to reload and you press {{keybind|Item Tertiary use}}, a reload animation begins playing. The animation is visible and audible to fellow employees, but not to entities,{{check}} and you can't fire until it's finished. But you can cancel it. When the animation is nearly done, the leftmost [[Ammo]] from your inventory is consumed and the number of shells in the Shotgun is increased, then the animation finishes (during which you still can't fire). Ammo will NOT be taken from a worn [[Belt bag]], nor the ground. There's no visual indication of whether the Shotgun is loaded, but you can diagnose it with sound cues: {| class="wikitable" |- ! Sound from pressing
{{keybind|Item Tertiary use|short=y}}
with no Ammo on hand, and ! sound from pressing
{{keybind|Item primary use|short=y}}
with the safety on !! means |- | [[File:ShotgunBlastFail.ogg]] || [[File:ShotgunBlastFail.ogg]] ! No shells left |- | [[File:ShotgunBlastFail.ogg]] || [[File:ShotgunSafetyOn.ogg]] ! 1 shell left |- | (silent) || [[File:ShotgunSafetyOn.ogg]] ! 2 shells left |} Or you could just grab [[Ammo]] and try loading it. It is technically a scrap item, meaning if all employees die, it will disappear from the ship. While it appears that you hold it with both hands, it does not make your [[hands full]], so in that sense it is not a two-handed item. Dropping a loaded shotgun with the safety OFF has a 5% chance of causing a shot to be fired, potentially killing the person who stepped on it/dropped it and others within the line of fire. When such a shotgun is in the world or a player's hands (but not when in inventory), it also has a 4% chance of misfiring every 37.15 seconds on average. (The timer is reset to a random number between 28 and 50 each check, with a 5% chance of it immediately checking again after 2 seconds instead.) Bug: Sometimes the shotgun will do no damage to enemies. If you are in a situation where the shotgun should've killed an enemy and it didn't, shooting the second shot will likely lead to the same result. Try repositioning the enemy to kill them, as the game might think the shot is being blocked by something. == Match-ups table == {{#invoke:Entities/AutoMatchupTable|matchupTable}} The Maneater has the special behavior of always taking only 1 damage from Shotguns in its adult form. (In its baby form, any attack will make it transform instead of dealing damage.) Tulip Snakes have the special behavior of dying in a single hit, despite ostensibly having 3 health. == References == {{navbox items}} [[Category:Tools]]

Mechanics.wikitext:
{{topicon Fandom}} [[Category:Gameplay_mechanics]] {{interlangbox}} {{Infobox game_data | image = LethalCompanyBanner.png | hour_length = 60 | hour_count = 18 | time_speed_multiplier = 1.4 | ship_leave_time = 11:57 PM | scrap_value_multiplier = 0.4 | scrap_amount_multiplier = 1.0 | map_size_multiplier = 1.5 | spawn_cooldown = 2h }} This article is meant to give experienced employees an in-depth view behind various game mechanics such as [[Entities|entity]] spawning, game cycles, [[Scrap|scrap]] collection and much more to come. All the values and information referenced here is up-to-date as of [[Version_81|Version 81]]. == Game variables == The infobox on the right provides global variables that define game values. These and other moon / entity / scrap specific variables will be referenced in ''[[w:Camel_case|camel case notation]]'' (e.g. 'Hour Length' will be referred as hourLength and a moon's 'Map Size Multiplier' will be referred as mapSizeMultiplier). Game variables are static, meaning they are the same for everyone and don't change over the course of a game. They might be adjusted by Zeekerss in future Lethal Company updates. == General principles == === Time calculations === Time in Lethal Company is defined by the variables hourLength and hoursCount. To be able to use time with curves, we need to work with ''normalized time''. To get the ''normalized time'', we have to divide current time by the total time (hourLength * hoursCount). Normalized time is a value between 0 and 1 and can be seen as the fraction of the day that has already passed. === Animation curves === Many mechanics in Lethal Company are based on Unity's [https://docs.unity3d.com/Manual/animeditor-AnimationCurves.html Animation Curves]. An animation curve is a mathematical function, that maps a value X (e.g. Time of Day) to another value Y (e.g. Spawn Chance). In the case of Lethal Company, the curves are interpolated using [[w:Cubic_Hermite_spline|Hermite cubic spline]] interpolation, which basically means that we are given set of input points, or keyframes (the black dots) and slopes that define how the curve is 'bent' around the points to then calculate the actual curve (the orange line). Here are the three Entity Spawn Curves of [[Experimentation]].
[[File:Experimentation_spawn_curve.png|x300px]]
And here is the spawn probability curve of a [[Baboon Hawk]].
[[File:Baboon_hawk_probability.png|x300px]]
When describing a value retrieved from a curve, the pseudo-function curve.eval(Y) will be used. == Entity spawning == Entity spawning in Lethal Company is quite complicated and dependent on a lot of variables. This section provides a very detailed description of the different spawning algorithms and the involved parameters. The entity spawning cycle runs once for indoor spawns at the start of the round, then once for every group at the start of each day, and then periodically after that until the day ends or the ship leaves early. The interval of the spawning cycle is dependent on the spawningCooldown. A cooldown of 2 (the default value) means that the game waits for 2 hours before the next spawn cycle is executed. Spawn waves use a slightly different time calculation than normalized time, referred to henceforth as ''spawn wave time''. Instead of current time, the variable currentHour is used, evaluating to (currentHour * hourLength) / (hourLength * hoursCount). currentHour, despite its name, is the hour before the next spawn wave, which occurs two hours later. This causes values in the curve to be sampled an hour later than they reasonably should be. === Spawning requirements === The spawning algorithm for an entity category is only executed if that category's '''Power Count''' is not yet at its maximum. For example, if the [[Bracken]] (Power Level 3) and a [[Thumper]] (Power Level 3) are already present on [[Assurance]], the moon's '''Max Indoor Power''' of 6 is reached and no additional indoor entities will spawn until the power count goes down. The spawning algorithm for an entity is also limited by the category's '''Diversity Power Count'''. For example, if the [[Feiopar]] (Diversity Power Level 2) and [[Baboon Hawk]] (Diversity Power Level 1) both spawned on [[March]], the moon's '''Max Outdoor Diversity Power''' of 3 is reached. More Feiopars and Baboon Hawks can spawn, but no other entities of a different species will spawn for the whole day. Killing all Feiopars or Baboon Hawks present will not deduct from this counter. === Spawning algorithm (Daytime Entities) === The amount of daytime entities spawned is based of the moon's '''Daytime Spawn Probability Curve''' and '''Daytime Spawn Deviation'''. The base spawn count is retrieved from evaluating the curve with the ''spawn wave time''. The actual spawn count is a number, randomly chosen in the following range and rounded to the nearest whole number. *'''Min Spawn Count:''' daytimeCurve.eval(spawnWaveTime) - daytimeSpawnDeviation *'''Max Spawn Count:''' daytimeCurve.eval(spawnWaveTime) + daytimeSpawnDeviation This spawn count is then clamped between 0 and 20. There will never be more than 20 daytime entities on the map at the same time. After the amount of entities to be spawned has been determined, the game determines which entities to spawn. For each entity slot, a list of spawn probabilities is created based on each entity's eligibility to be spawned. The eligibility is calculated based on the following factors. *'''Max Count:''' The entity's spawn count wouldn't exceed its own '''Max Count''' *'''Power Level:''' The added entity's power level wouldn't exceed the moon's '''Max Daytime Power''' *'''Latest Spawn Time:''' The entity's latest spawn time has not yet passed (e.g. [[Roaming_Locusts|Roaming Locusts]] stop spawning after 7:41 PM) The spawn chance for ineligible entities is set to 0. Eligible entities get their current moon spawn chance multiplied by their own '''Spawn Probability Curve''' value that is based on the Time of Day. If the entity uses '''Spawn Count Falloff''' and therefore has a '''Spawn Count Falloff''' curve, the spawn chance also gets multiplied by that curve value, evaluated for the amount of already spawned entities of the same type. Finally, the game picks a random weighted index from the list of eligible entities. The selected entity gets spawned immediately at one of the predefined daytime entity spawn points. === Spawning algorithm (Outdoor Entities) === The amount of outdoor entities spawned is based of the moon's '''Outdoor Spawn Probability Curve''' and '''Outdoor Spawn Deviation'''. The base spawn count is retrieved from evaluating the curve with the ''spawn wave time''. The actual spawn count is also a number, randomly chosen from a range and rounded to the nearest whole number. The minimum count is dependent on how many days are left until the quota must be reached (daysUntilDeadline). The more days have passed, the more likely it becomes for entities to spawn. *'''Min Spawn Count:''' outdoorCurve.eval(spawnWaveTime) + abs(daysUntilDeadline-3) / 1.6 - 3 *'''Max Spawn Count:''' outdoorCurve.eval(spawnWaveTime) + 3 This spawn count is then clamped between minOutsideEnemiesToSpawn and 20. The variable minOutsideEnemiesToSpawn is set to 0 by default but can change to a predefined value if the current moon is [[Weather|eclipsed]]. Each moon has its own minOutsideEnemiesToSpawn during eclipses which can be found on their individual pages. 1 spawn is added to the minimum and maximum if the moon is a challenge moon. After the amount of entities to be spawned has been determined, the game determines which entities to spawn. For each entity slot, a list of spawn probabilities is created based on each entity's eligibility to be spawned. The eligibility is calculated based on the following factors. *'''Max Count:''' The entity's spawn count wouldn't exceed its own '''Max Count''' *'''Power Level:''' The added entity's power level wouldn't exceed the moon's '''Max Outdoor Power''' *'''Diversity Power Level:''' The added entity's diversity level wouldn't exceed the moon's '''Max Outdoor Diversity Power''' *'''Latest Spawn Time:''' The entity's latest spawn time has not yet passed The spawn chance for ineligible entities is set to 0. Eligible entities get their current moon spawn chance multiplied by their own '''Spawn Probability Curve''' value that is evaluated for the current Time of Day. If the entity uses '''Spawn Count Falloff''' and therefore has a '''Spawn Count Falloff''' curve, the spawn chance also gets multiplied by that curve value, evaluated for the amount of already spawned entities of the same type. Finally, the game picks a random weighted index from the list of eligible entities. The selected entity gets spawned immediately at one of the predefined outdoor entity spawn points. === Spawning algorithm (Indoor Entities) === The spawn algorithm for indoor entities is more complicated than the previous two, since indoor enemies don't spawn instantly, and are instead assigned a vent and a timer that specifies when the entity will be spawned from that vent. The amount of indoor entities spawned is based off the moon's '''Indoor Spawn Probability Curve''' and '''Indoor Spawn Deviation'''. The base spawn count is retrieved from evaluating the curve with the ''spawn wave time''. The actual spawn count is again, a number that is randomly chosen from a range and rounded to the nearest whole number. As with outdoor entities, the minimum count is dependent on how many days are left until the quota must be reached (daysUntilDeadline). The more days have passed, the more likely it becomes for indoor entities to spawn. *'''Min Spawn Count:''' (indoorCurve.eval(spawnWaveTime) - 1) + abs(daysUntilDeadline-3) / 1.6 - indoorSpawnDeviation *'''Max Spawn Count:''' (indoorCurve.eval(spawnWaveTime) - 1) + indoorSpawnDeviation This spawn count is then clamped between minEnemiesToSpawn and 20 or the amount of free vents on the map. Every selected entity must be able to be assigned to a free vent later. 1 spawn is added to the minimum and maximum if the moon is a challenge moon. The variable minEnemiesToSpawn is set to 0 by default but can change to a predefined value if the current moon is [[Weather|eclipsed]], or to 2 (with a 70% chance) when an [[apparatus]] is collected. Each moon has its own minEnemiesToSpawn during eclipses which can be found on their individual pages. In addition to this, before clamping occurs, if ''one day has already passed'' and minEnemiesToSpawn ''is still set to 0'' (the current moon is ''not eclipsed'', and ''apparatus has not increased spawn rates''), minEnemiesToSpawn will be set to 1 if one of the following conditions is met. * Percentage of scrap found in the current round is greater than or equal to the profit quota and it's after 11:24 AM (intended to be greater than 80% of quota, but a typo causes percentage to round down to a multiple of 100%) * Percentage of scrap found in the current round is greater than 65% of the total scrap value on the map * At least 5 rounds have passed (total, not necessarily consecutively) where more than 30 credits worth of scrap was collected and no players died. If a player dies at any point, the count starts over from 0. After the amount of entities to be spawned has been determined, the game determines which entities to spawn. For each entity slot, a list of spawn probabilities is created based on each entity's eligibility to be spawned. The eligibility is calculated based on the following factors. *'''Max Count:''' The entity's spawn count wouldn't exceed its own '''Max Count''' *'''Power Level:''' The added entity's power level wouldn't exceed the moon's '''Max Indoor Power''' *'''Diversity Power Level:''' The added entity's diversity level wouldn't exceed the moon's '''Max Indoor Diversity Power''' *'''Latest Spawn Time:''' The entity's latest spawn time has not yet passed The spawn chance for ineligible entities is set to 0. Eligible entities get their current moon spawn chance multiplied by their own '''Spawn Probability Curve''' value that is evaluated for the current time of day. Entities that use '''Spawn Count Falloff''' and therefore have a '''Spawn Count Falloff''' curve, also have their spawn chance multiplied by that curve's value, evaluated for the amount of already spawned entities of that same type. Finally, the game picks a random weighted index from the list of eligible entities. The selected entity is then assigned to a randomly picked free vent on the map alongside a randomly determined ''spawn delay''. The spawn delay is clamped between the current time and the start of the next spawn cycle. An indoor entity's spawningDelay defines how far in advance the vent crawling sound will be played. The sound starts off quiet and gets louder over time. Once the spawningDelay is over the sound stops, the vent opens and the assigned entity spawns in front of the vent. The vent then resets and is ready to be assigned again in the next spawn cycle. ===Entity Infestations=== At the start of every day, the game rolls a 0.4% chance. If the current date is October 23rd (Lethal Company's anniversary), then it also rolls a ~4.545% chance. If either rolls succeed (~4.927% chance), then the current day may have an indoor infestation.{{ref code|RoundManager.RefreshEnemiesList}} There are currently two types of infestations. If [[Nutcracker|Nutcrackers]] are on the list of indoor entities and a 25% roll succeeds, the facility will have a Nutcracker infestation. Otherwise, if [[Hoarding Bug|Hoarding Bugs]] are on the list of indoor entities, the facility will have a Hoarding Bug infestation. If neither conditions are met, the infestation is ignored.{{ref code|RoundManager.RefreshEnemiesList}} On infestation days, the respective entity is marked as an infestation entity, the indoor power level is increased to either 30 (for Hoarding Bugs) or 20 (for Nutcrackers){{ref code|RoundManager.RefreshEnemiesList}}, and the 2 spawns are added to the wave{{ref code|RoundManager.PlotOutEnemiesForNextHour}}. In addition, all standard spawning probabilities (including probabilities of 0) and probability modifiers are ignored, and the probability is set to 100 for the infestation entity and multiplied by 0.075 for every other entity. Other things that prevent an entity from spawning (such as max spawns) can still prevent an entity from spawning.{{ref code|RoundManager.AssignRandomEnemyToVent}} As such, until the max spawns of the infestation entity are reached, almost all spawns will consist of that entity, though occasionally, other entities can spawn as well. Afterwards, every other entity will spawn with equal probability until the power level cap is reached. Infestations only affect indoor spawns. === Special Enemies === The '''special enemy system''' is used to prioritize specific enemy spawns on a moon during each spawn wave. After normal spawn weights have been calculated, the special enemy's weight is set to a weight that matches the defined percentage. If the percentage is 100%, every spawn wave is artificially forced to have at least one of this defined enemy, unless it has already reached its maximum count, or the moon's indoor power level has been reached. A special enemy does not have to exist for every moon. This system is only currently utilized by the [[Cadaver Growth]]s on [[Adamance]], with a percentage of 100%. == Scrap spawning == {{for|information on days that spawn only one scrap type|Single scrap day}} === Rarity === When spawning in another scrap item, the game uses an RNG combined with the 'rarity table' for that particular moon. These tables are lists of the possible scrap items and their relative rarities, which are simple whole numbers. (You can think of these like "tickets" in a raffle or lottery; having 2 makes it twice as likely to be chosen as having 1.)
Thus, we can calculate the chances of each type of scrap getting spawned as its relative rarity divided by the total of all the rarity values in the table. It's these chances (as percentages) that you'll see in tables like this one across other articles: {{#invoke:Moons/AutoTables|lootTable|Dine}} (The calculations are handled by [[Module:Rarities]], and the datamined rarity tables are stored in subpages of that. See [[Project:WikiAutomated]] for more info.) == Quota == Quota is increased every four days by a few random factors, the number of quotas passed (quotaCount), and furniture luck. An integer value, dubbed the ''quota to add'', is set to (quotaCount * (quotaCount / 16)), increased by 1, then clamped between 0 and 10,000. The randomizer curve value, dubbed the ''curve sample'', is evaluated as a random percentage minus furniture luck * 1.5. Then, the quota to add is multiplied by 100 and randomizerCurve.eval(curveSample) + 1. This is then added to the current quota amount, then clamped between 0 and 1 billion.{{ref code|TimeOfDay.SetNewProfitQuota}} Overtime credits is decided by subtracting '''total sold - quota''', dividing by 5, and adding or subtracting multiples of 15 dependent on the day sold. On 3 days left, 30 will be added; then 15 added for 2 days, none for 1 day, and 15 subtracted for 0 days. The formula in game is as follows: * (total sold - quota) / 5 + (15 * daysUntilDeadline) where daysUntilDeadline is -1 for 0 days left and 2 for 3 days left. After these values are calculated and set, amount of quota fulfilled is reset, the time until deadline is set to totalTime * 4, present furniture items are added to the luck counter for next quota, and the flag for [[HUD#Advertisements|advertisements]] is reset. === Furniture luck === Furniture luck is a system used primarily by quota increase randomization, but is also used to decide [[Vain Shroud]] growth. Each furniture item has a specified luck value, and when the new quota is set, furniture luck is updated to include the percentages of all furniture items that have been present since last quota. Here is the table for each furniture item and its luck: {| class="wikitable sortable" ! Name !! Luck |- | [[Classic painting]] || 0.60% |- | [[Cozy lights]] || 0.50% |- | [[Disco ball]] || 6.00% |- | [[Dog house]] || 0.70% |- | [[Electric chair]] || 1.20% |- | [[Fridge]] || 1.00% |- | [[Goldfish]] || 0.60% |- | [[Inverse Teleporter]] || 0.40% |- | [[Jack-o-Lantern]] || 1.20% |- | [[Loud horn]] || 0.25% |- | [[Microwave]] || 1.00% |- | [[Plushie pajama man]] || 0.30% |- | [[Record player]] || 0.50% |- | [[Romantic table]] || 0.50% |- | [[Shower]] || 1.50% |- | [[Signal Translator]] || -1.20% |- | [[Sofa chair]] || 0.80% |- | [[Table]] || 0.40% |- | [[Television]] || 2.00% |- | [[Toilet]] || 1.00% |- | [[Welcome mat]] || 0.30% |} Furniture must be kept on the ship and unstored for the whole quota for it to factor into luck for the next quota. This is ''not'' the case for items with negative luck, which have a specific case where its luck is always counted if it is ever unstored during the whole quota. This is to prevent exploitation by removing it before the new quota is calculated and negating the negative luck. For Vain Shroud growth, luck is not recalculated; instead, it is read from the previous quota's luck value, as assigning a new quota is the only time it is recalculated. == Damage == {{main|Damage}} ==Notes== Possibly a bug. The exact rolls are 4/1000 (0.4%) and 5/110 (~4.54545%). This does not modify minEnemiesToSpawn and as such, things such as pulling the apparatus (which only sets minEnemiesToSpawn to 2 if it is less than 2) can further increase the minimum spawns. The probability increase based on the current interior still happens, but is capped at 100. ==References==
